learning_objectives: Define cybersecurity and its core principles
Identify the CIA triad and its significance

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Introduction to Cybersecurity. This course establishes the foundational knowledge you need to understand how digital systems are protected against threats.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live walkthrough of a simulated phishing attack and how defenses intercept it

review.value: Confidentiality ensures data is accessible only to authorized parties; Integrity ensures data is not altered; Availability ensures systems are operational when needed
Layered security approach where multiple controls protect assets so that if one fails, others remain

class_schedule.topic: Cyber Threat Landscape and Attack Vectors
learning_objectives: Classify common types of cyber attacks
Differentiate between threat actors and their motivations
Understand the cyber kill chain model
readings: Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain whitepaper

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding who attacks systems, why they do it, and how they do it is critical to building effective defenses.
key_concepts: Malware types: viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware
Nation-state vs. criminal vs. hacktivist motivations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of a real-world breach timeline using the cyber kill chain model

review.value: Reconnaissance, Weaponization, Delivery, Exploitation, Installation, Command & Control, Actions on Objectives
Manipulating human psychology to bypass technical controls; includes phishing, pretexting, baiting, and tailgating

learning_objectives: Explain least privilege and separation of duties
Compare access control models (DAC, MAC, RBAC)

lecture_notes.introduction: Access control is the gatekeeper of cybersecurity. Today we examine the models and principles that govern who can access what.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring Linux file permissions and Windows Active Directory group policies
setup_requirements: Linux VM with multiple user accounts
Windows Server VM with Active Directory

review.value: Users and processes should have only the minimum permissions necessary to perform their functions
Access permissions are assigned to roles, and users are assigned to roles, simplifying management of large organizations

learning_objectives: Identify common network security devices and their functions
Explain the role of IDS and IPS systems

lecture_notes.introduction: Networks are the highways of digital communication, and securing them is one of the most critical tasks in cybersecurity.
key_concepts: Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) vs. Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring iptables rules on Linux and observing traffic with Wireshark

review.value: Tracks the state of active connections and makes decisions based on the context of traffic, not just individual packets
IDS monitors and alerts on suspicious traffic; IPS actively blocks malicious traffic inline

learning_objectives: Distinguish between symmetric and asymmetric encryption
Apply basic cryptographic concepts to real scenarios

lecture_notes.introduction: Cryptography is the mathematical backbone of cybersecurity. It protects data at rest, in transit, and in use.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Encrypting and decrypting files using OpenSSL, generating key pairs, and verifying digital signatures

review.value: Symmetric uses one shared key for both encryption and decryption (fast); Asymmetric uses a key pair (public/private) enabling secure key exchange
One-way function producing a fixed-length digest; used for integrity verification, password storage, and digital signatures

class_schedule.topic: Security Operations and Incident Response Overview
learning_objectives: Identify key roles in a Security Operations Center
Understand log management and SIEM concepts

lecture_notes.introduction: Even the best defenses can be breached. How an organization detects, responds to, and recovers from incidents defines its resilience.
key_concepts: Incident response phases: Preparation, Detection, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Lessons Learned
Business continuity and disaster recovery basics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walking through a simulated incident using Splunk to correlate logs and identify the attack vector

review.value: Six phases that provide a structured approach to handling security incidents from preparation through lessons learned
Security Information and Event Management aggregates logs from multiple sources, correlates events, and generates alerts on suspicious activity

questions.question: A company stores customer medical records in a database. An attacker modifies several records without detection. Which element of the CIA triad has been violated, and what specific control would best prevent this?
questions.answer: Integrity has been violated. Implementing cryptographic hashing (e.g., HMAC-SHA256) on database records with periodic integrity checks, combined with database activity monitoring and write-ahead logging, would detect and prevent unauthorized modifications.

rubric.key: Explains how the control prevents the attack

questions.question: Map the following attack scenario to the Cyber Kill Chain phases: An attacker scrapes employee LinkedIn profiles, crafts a spear-phishing email with a malicious PDF, the PDF exploits Adobe Reader to install a RAT, which connects to a C2 server, and eventually exfiltrates the company's trade secrets.
questions.answer: Reconnaissance: LinkedIn scraping. Weaponization: Crafting the malicious PDF. Delivery: Sending the spear-phishing email. Exploitation: Adobe Reader vulnerability triggered. Installation: RAT installed on victim machine. Command & Control: RAT connects to C2 server. Actions on Objectives: Trade secret exfiltration.

questions.question: A hospital needs to ensure that nurses can view patient vitals but cannot modify treatment plans, while doctors can do both. Which access control model is most appropriate, and how would you implement it?
questions.answer: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is most appropriate. Create a 'Nurse' role with read-only permissions on patient vitals and treatment plans, and a 'Doctor' role with read/write permissions on both. Assign users to roles based on their job function. This provides scalable, auditable access management aligned with the principle of least privilege.

rubric.key: Identifies RBAC as the correct model

questions.question: Explain why a system should use bcrypt or Argon2 instead of plain SHA-256 for storing user passwords, even though SHA-256 is a cryptographically secure hash function.
questions.answer: SHA-256 is fast by design, which makes it vulnerable to brute-force and rainbow table attacks when used for password hashing. bcrypt and Argon2 are deliberately slow (key stretching), include a salt to prevent rainbow tables, and have a configurable work factor that can be increased as hardware improves. Argon2 additionally provides memory-hard resistance against GPU-based cracking.

rubric.key: Explains SHA-256 speed as a vulnerability
Mentions configurable work factor or memory-hardness

major_assignments.title: Security Assessment Report
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive security assessment of a provided virtual network environment. Identify vulnerabilities, classify threats, and produce a professional security assessment report with remediation recommendations.
objectives: Apply the CIA triad to evaluate real system security
Identify and classify vulnerabilities using CVSS scoring
Map discovered threats to the MITRE ATT&CK framework
Produce a professional-quality security assessment report
deliverables: Detailed findings report with CVSS scores (5-8 pages)

criteria: All critical and high vulnerabilities identified
Comprehensive identification and analysis of all vulnerabilities
Professional-quality report suitable for executive presentation
Actionable remediation plan with realistic implementation timeline
Demonstrates mastery of foundational cybersecurity concepts

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of cybersecurity concepts and threat landscape knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on exercises in vulnerability scanning, access control, and basic security tools

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive security assessment report demonstrating analytical skills

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering foundational cybersecurity principles and threat analysis

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all course material including practical scenarios

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. All written reports must be individual work. Code sharing is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Unauthorized access to systems outside the lab environment is strictly prohibited and may result in expulsion. All work must be original.
course_policies.lab_safety: Students must only perform security testing within the designated lab environment. Any unauthorized scanning or exploitation of university or external networks will result in immediate course failure and disciplinary action.
